does: This paper identifies and exploits a fundamental vulnerability in machine unlearning systems by demonstrating that attackers can catastrophically degrade model performance by submitting unlearning requests for data that was never in the original training set. The authors develop white-box and black-box attack algorithms that manipulate the unlearning process to corrupt model weights and features. Through extensive evaluation on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet using standard unlearning methods, they show that adversarial unlearning requests reduce test accuracy to near-random levels (3.6% on CIFAR-10 and 0.4% on ImageNet in white-box settings), demonstrating the severity of this attack vector. The paper also evaluates existing verification mechanisms designed to authenticate unlearning requests and reveals their insufficient robustness against determined adversaries.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of machine unlearning, which addresses privacy concerns by enabling selective removal of training data from deployed models. It responds to recent progress in unlearning algorithms that approximate data deletion without full retraining. The paper extends adversarial robustness research by introducing a new attack surface that exploits the assumption underlying unlearning deployment—that removal requests correspond to legitimate training data. It connects to broader security research on model poisoning and adversarial perturbations, but targets the unlearning interface specifically. The work also engages with verification and authentication mechanisms studied in privacy-preserving machine learning, exposing gaps between theoretical defenses and practical circumvention.
enables: This research opens investigation into defending unlearning systems against adversarial requests, motivating development of robust verification mechanisms that can reliably distinguish between legitimate and malicious data removal requests. It highlights the need for unlearning algorithms that maintain both privacy guarantees and resilience against abuse. Future work can explore cryptographic or zero-knowledge approaches to verify data membership without revealing training data characteristics. The findings suggest broader implications for deployable privacy-preserving systems, indicating that mechanisms assuming honest participation may require adversarial hardening. This work also motivates theoretical analysis of what properties enable safe unlearning under adversarial conditions, potentially leading to certified defenses against malicious unlearning requests.
